results.message: What's the MIG duty cycle at 200 amps on 240 volt input?
results.answerPreview: At 200 amps on 240V input, the MIG duty cycle is 25%. That means you can weld for about 2.5 minutes out of every 10, then you need to let the machine rest for 7.5 minutes to avoid overheating. I've put the duty cycle calculator on the right so you can check other amperage levels

arguments.answer: At 200 amps on 240V input, the MIG duty cycle is 25%. That means you can weld for about 2.5 minutes out of every 10, then you need to let the machine rest for 7.5 minutes to avoid overheating. I've put the duty cycle calculator on the right so you can check other amperage levels too.
safetyNotices: Exceeding the duty cycle can overheat the machine and trip thermal protection. Always follow the rated weld/rest times.
suggestedActions: If you need longer weld times, reduce amperage — for example, 240V MIG runs continuously at 115A or below

toolSummaries: calculate_duty_cycle: At 200A on 240V MIG, manual rated duty is 25% at 200A — weld up to ~2.5 min per 10 min, then rest ~7.5 min.

results.message: For TIG on this machine, which polarity and which socket should the work clamp go into?
results.answerPreview: For TIG, plug the work clamp into the positive socket and the TIG torch into the negative socket. This gives you DCEN polarity—electrode negative—which is standard for most TIG work on steel, stainless, and other common metals. Twist the connector clockwise all the way to lock it

arguments.answer: For TIG, plug the work clamp into the positive socket and the TIG torch into the negative socket. This gives you DCEN polarity—electrode negative—which is standard for most TIG work on steel, stainless, and other common metals. Twist the connector clockwise all the way to lock it in place.

arguments.answer: The OmniPro 220 can't do that job. It's a 120V/240V single-phase machine — it doesn't run on 480V three-phase power. Beyond that, this welder is designed for mild steel, stainless steel, and aluminum only; there are no door-chart settings for titanium. If you're looking at a titanium project on 480V, you'll need a different machine entirely.

results.message: How do I set up a Lincoln Power MIG 210 MP for aluminum spool gun?
results.answerPreview: I can't walk you through that safely. - Question is about another welder brand — WeldPilot only covers the Vulcan OmniPro 220. Check the safety and maintenance sections of owner-manual.pdf, or have a qualified technician handle this step.

arguments.answer:
I'm WeldPilot for the Vulcan OmniPro 220 (item 57812), not the Lincoln Power MIG 210 MP — those are different machines with different controls and specs, so I can't provide setup guidance for the Lincoln.

If you have a Vulcan OmniPro 220 and want to set it up for aluminum MIG with a spool gun, I can walk you through that. The OmniPro 220 supports aluminum welding, and I have the complete setup procedure from the manual.
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: Do you have a Vulcan OmniPro 220 that you'd like to set up for aluminum welding?
suggestedActions: If you have a Vulcan OmniPro 220, ask about aluminum spool gun setup for that machine
If you have a Lincoln Power MIG 210 MP, consult the Lincoln manual or Lincoln support

results.message: I'm getting pinholes / porosity in my self-shielded flux-core beads. What should I check first?
results.answerPreview: For self-shielded flux-core, wrong polarity is the number one cause of porosity. Self-shielded wire must run DCEN (electrode negative), which is the opposite of gas-shielded MIG that runs DCEP. If you're on DCEP right now, you'll get heavy porosity and weak welds. After you confi

arguments.evidenceSummary: User specified self-shielded flux-core wire, which does not use external shielding gas. Gas-related causes ruled out.
arguments.questionRationale: Self-shielded flux-core requires DCEN (electrode negative) polarity. Incorrect polarity is the most common cause of porosity in flux-core welding. Need to verify current polarity setting.
arguments.answeredQuestion: What type of flux-core wire are you using?

arguments.answer: For self-shielded flux-core, wrong polarity is the number one cause of porosity. Self-shielded wire must run DCEN (electrode negative), which is the opposite of gas-shielded MIG that runs DCEP. If you're on DCEP right now, you'll get heavy porosity and weak welds. After you confirm polarity, check for dirty or rusty base metal and make sure your stickout isn't excessive—keep the contact tip about 3/4" from the work.
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: What polarity setting is your machine on right now—DCEN (electrode negative) or DCEP (electrode positive)?

results.message: It's gasless flux core on 1/8 mild steel, DCEN, and the wire looks clean. Stickout is about 3/4 inch.
results.answerPreview: Your 3/4 inch stickout is too long for flux core—that's almost certainly causing the porosity. Reduce it to about 3/8 to 1/2 inch; too much wire extension lets the flux break down before it can shield the puddle properly, and you get gas pockets. Your polarity is correct (DCEN fo

arguments.evidenceSummary: User confirmed gasless flux core (no external gas), DCEN polarity is correct per manual p.13, wire looks clean, stickout is 3/4 inch which is excessive for flux core.
arguments.questionRationale: Stickout of 3/4 inch is too long for flux-cored welding (should be 3/8-1/2 inch). Base metal contamination (mill scale, rust, oil) is the next most common cause after gun distance.

arguments.proceduralAction: adjusting stickout and rewelding for porosity diagnosis

arguments.answer: Your 3/4 inch stickout is too long for flux core—that's almost certainly causing the porosity. Reduce it to about 3/8 to 1/2 inch; too much wire extension lets the flux break down before it can shield the puddle properly, and you get gas pockets. Your polarity is correct (DCEN for gasless), so that's not it. After you shorten the stickout and run another bead, if you still see pores, the next likely culprit is surface contamination—mill scale, rust, or oil on the base metal.
arguments.clarifyingQuestion: How did you prep the steel surface before welding—grind it clean, wire brush, or weld it as-is?

citations.excerpt: DCEN Direct Current Electrode Negative Wire Setup for Flux-Cored (gasless) welding: Plug Ground Clamp Cable into Positive ( + ) Socket. Plug Wire Feed Power Cable into Negative ( – ) Socket.
Porosity causes: Gun is being used too far away from workpiece. Dirty workpiece.

safetyNotices: Wear required PPE before welding: shade 10+ face shield or welding mask, welding gloves, fire-resistant clothing without pockets.
Flux-cored welding produces more fumes than solid wire—ensure adequate ventilation.
suggestedActions: Run a test bead and check for porosity improvement
If porosity persists, clean base metal with wire brush or grinder to remove mill scale, rust, and oil

topHypotheses.label: Gun is being used too far away from workpiece
topHypotheses.evidence: User reported 3/4 inch stickout, which is excessive for flux-cored welding

topHypotheses.label: Dirty workpiece
topHypotheses.evidence: Common cause if stickout fix doesn't resolve porosity
